sentences.text: Well, what I-- I had to add about, uh-- the-- or-- Joe's brothers and sisters, as an equal-- equal citizen, there was no such thing as-- well, I- I- I'd be wrong to say that there was no-- no-- some sign of anti-semitism hidden there, because it is-- a-a-always-- always infiltrated.
So what it-- uh, has absolutely n-n-no effect.
Sometimes-- I remember one time where a teacher came in from-- from-- from Romania, from Transylvania.
See-- many escaped after the First World War, and came to Romania-- occupation.
And many, many, many of the intellectuals-- and in all, who was there? -two teachers, and one of-- one of the bellmen in the Catholic church, and he was really wild man.
He hated everybody.
And he especially hated the Jews.
But sometimes, sometimes, it broke out from him-- but that was no-- that was no-- no-- ground for it.
No-- nobody fell for it.
We are happy with that, and we-- we are really happy that the Jews go in the store, and- and we have-- a-- a place to go for grocery, and for yard stuff, and so on.
So it was really an objectively-- it was an-- an assimilation on the part of the-- of the Jewish-- Jewish people.
And they had no- no special-- special desires, or anything.
They were satisfied that as they- as they were.
You see-- they took-- they placed and they served the peasantry, so to-- so to say.
And it was agricultural, see, and uh.
And, well-- well, there were in the- in the village surroundings, the- the big land estate-- there were a few big, uh, landowners with 2,000 acres-- and-- and there was only 6,000 acres.
And so-- so-- and they were the ones, then, who brought out some anti- anti-Semitism.
But, well, it wasn't anything more than the Christian Hungarian landowners.

And as a matter of fact, the people actually felt this-- this atmosphere that the servants on-- on the Jewish estates are better treated than the ser-- the same kind of servants-- the horse drivers, and the oxen drivers, and then other-- than on-- on the Christian Hungarians there.
No-- these-- these-- these are the facts.
They never wanted to-- or wanted to- to admit it, but that's-- that was the de facto situation.
Well, because of my-- of the activities of my father, who was in the leading-- leading personnel in the 1919 socialist revolution in the village.
They called it the-- the council was called the Directorium.
And, uh, and they were-- were very close contact-- contact with the provincial city, [NON-ENGLISH], you see.
And there, a famous, real upstanding Jewish fellow, uh, Tibo-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] it just slipped my-- my mind.
Who was the second in command, next to Bela Kun-- Szamuely-- Tibor Szamuely.
I saw him personally, on the first [LAUGHS] May Day, when Hungarian revolution came.
Following the-- the red flag, it was-- it was so long that it went out about 10-- 10 meters.
And Tibor Szamuely was at-- at the front, leading the May Day demonstration-- it was first such open May Day [NON-ENGLISH] day in the-- in the history of Hungary, in 1919.

No, no-- be-- because of-- because of his-- because of his philosophy, his political outlook-- well, he was kept out on the-- on the end, mind you, in 1919, when the revolution was-- had been suppressed in blood.
Well, he escaped with some other people, and his closest friend was the- the village-- the primash-- the gypsy primash-- [LAUGHS] violenced prima-- and they-- they escaped into Austria.
And-- and that's where-- because my father came from there, he knew his way around.
And then-- and they were able to get a- get a job, and so on.
But the Terror-- the [NON-ENGLISH], who was-- well, this was called the White Terror, the [NON-ENGLISH] is White Terror, supplied the revolution.
And it was so severe, and so brutal-- and anti-Semite, mind you [INAUDIBLE].

That the international community-- through the League of Nations, it was functioning already-- declared an amnesty, and they forced [INAUDIBLE] to declare amnesty to the-- to the-- to the refugees, and so on.
And that was in 1921, when father-- my father came back from Austria, and rejoined the family, again.
That I remember-- I remember-- very-- very vividly.
And then-- then he was a marked man, you see.
The [INAUDIBLE] army-- that, uh, brutal force, suppressive force-- [INAUDIBLE] brutal and-- around the globe-- the Hungarian [INAUDIBLE] army.
Well, he visited and-- and he-- and he aggravated my father.
They were always on his-- they shadowed him all the time, you see.

And that was a life on the [INAUDIBLE], already, [INAUDIBLE] And in the first-- first years, I remember, in school, you are-- you are fingered by- by your schoolmates, that all-- that all these communists and Bolsheviks, and then the [INAUDIBLE] or Reds, and so on.
It was because, uh, one of these teacher who came as a refugee from Transylvania, escaping the Romanians, became, uh, one of my teachers in Protestant school, school you see.
And, well, he knew.
He hated my father.
And not only my father, but the-- the-- the [INAUDIBLE].
He was a really-- a rough, brutal guy-- guy, you see.
He beat us up-- and specially me, because he knew who-- who-- who I was.

And then this-- this kind of aggravation, and then finally, my-- I had my uncle, my brother-- my mother's brother, older brother, who came to Canada-- immigrated to Canada in 1927, took up a homestead in northern Alberta, so- so he was out.
And then my father and mother decided, well, the best way for us to leave the country, because then-- then-- there was a sort of a [INAUDIBLE] movement, you see, to boycott me.
But they had to come to him, because he was the only-- only harness maker.
And he was--he was making all good, as I mentioned before.
He was able to send two of his children to pub-- to high school.
It-- it-- it was something.
But then--
Well, it's not-- it wasn't an escape, but as- but as I said, my father was under such-- such pressure, and the- and the- and the village, village, uh, the village-- not-- not the clerk.
But there is not a name in English-- who-- who-- was the head of the--
No-- well, not-- not-- not the mayor, which it has another, but it's like-- like the mayor in the- in the cities.
He was the- he was the head of the-- head of the village.
He was- he was also an escapee from-- from-- from-- Transyl-- from Romania.
So these people hated-- hated everybody, because the Romanian army was the main force-- main force, which was used by [NON-ENGLISH] to suppress the revolution.
So did everything added together.
And then-- and that all-- all this burden was-- was on-- on my father, until he felt we had enough.
And let's go-- let's go to Canada.
Well, yes, my-- my mother-- since being a, uh, peasant girl, the, uh, her father sold-- had about four-- four hectares of land.
But [INAUDIBLE] four hectares of land, but it was-- and when they parents, my great-grandparents died, and I remained there with the two sisters and one brother.
And they divided up-- my mother had in there about 1 and 1/2 hectares.
And it was really good money-- good money at the time.
But with a family of six, you had to have money.
And, uh, uh, uh, um, mother had some-- some relatives with a little bit of money, and she borrowed it from them.

And it took about four years before we could-- with dollar and dollar could send it back, because we arrived in-- in the spring in May-- May 1st, we landed in Edmonton, and we were up to next [INAUDIBLE] had in a Great Depression.
And with a family like that, and she talked to a-- to a CPR, get quarter section, which was 106 [INAUDIBLE] and all forest.
We had to clear the land, even for the-- for the one-room-- one-room house.
No, no, no, no, on-- on-- on the contrary.
It took also, it was a-- it was a-- a-- calvary for-- for my father to-- to acquire.
They wanted to get through to him, but when he applied for a-- for a-- for an exit visa from-- from the village, then-- then they held it back, you see.
It was just a revenge, you know, to teach-- just to torture him, you see, because it was so-- so-- much-- so much hatred against.
First, he was-- he was-- a-- a-- he was classified as a Slovak.
And he spoke first-- he spoke German, he spoke Slovak.
And since that promise was a right border with Poland, she spoke-- he spoke Polish.
And then came down to the-- came into Hungary, he spoke Hungarian, and he learned some-- a few words in Russian, in Russian, in front of you he works in the Italian front.
[LAUGHS]
So he was a-- a highly-- a highly educated man, otherwise.
But as I say, it was so badly controlled.
[INAUDIBLE]-- actually, he was terrorized.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] And then-- and then-- they-- they hated him because-- because of this.
Where do you-- where did you go?
To what country did you go?
To-- to the what port?
Well, that was-- that was the usual one.
Then through Austria, Switzerland, Switzerland, and they touched down on Germany into Belgium-- to Antwerp.
We sailed from-- from Antwerp.
And we-- we were docked in South Hampton, in En-- South England.
And then in St.
John, New Brunswick.
And from there, the immigrant train-- still the wooden-- the wooden benches, and so on.
It was a really-- you know, the immigration came, and it [INAUDIBLE] the immigrant-- the immigrant trains [INAUDIBLE].
Then we-- we, uh, uh, reached into the-- into the entire Canadian Shield, and then my mother saw this wilderness and [INAUDIBLE].
She almost committed suicide.
Told my father, this is the place that you brought us?
He said, well I didn't know where we were going there-- but, you know we had to, we had to, we had to leave there.
And so--
No, well, we landed in-- we landed in-- in Winnipeg.
That was the central focal point.
Because we came with the CPR.
And there was-- there was-- there was an agent who was waiting-- was waiting-- was in Winnipeg, a Joseph Swartz.
And he was-- he was a settler, sort of doing-- working for the CPR.
And then he then led us out to-- out to Edmonton.
And then-- then he landed us-- handed us, the whole family, over to a CPR agent.
Then they took us southwest, for 40-- 40 miles, southwest from Edmonton, where the CPR-- I don't know if you know the history of Canada.
When the CPR built the railways, by agreement with the Canadian-- Canadian government, they inject a wee bit of-- of sod.
[LAUGHS] The, uh, state, uh, reserves so much land along the railway tracks, some-- in some places, 10-- 10 miles on in each way.
And where the CPR built a railroad-- through like-- like in the prairies, I think that it's five or six miles on both-- both sides of the railway.
That was then donated by the Canadian government to the CPR.
It's-- when it then, it aligned, lead into the-- into the Rocky Mountains-- there's a different story, again.
But there, too, they-- they had certain rights, through the CPR.
So that's a-- that's how it work.
And on this land, it was-- you know, the whole-- the whole prairie-- Canada was divided up to-- up to this, uh, uh, uh, sections.
And the quarter section was 160 acres-- that was the smallest.
And it was-- it was forest-- that you could-- you couldn't see see the God from it.
[LAUGHS] And thus we started to-- to-- work on it.
Cut the-- cut the trees, and what.
But-- but-- then we leave it soon.
Fir-- our first job was to go to a Polish farm, and there was already cultivating about 120-- 20 acres.
Our first job was planting potatoes for that farmer, and my-- my little sister-- well, then she was-- she was hired by the same-- same farmer as a domestic girl.
Because there was only the mother and two-- two brothers around that farm.
So that was our first job.
1930, yeah.
We arrived in Edmonton, 1st of May.
And we were put in-- well, they had these-- these immigrants depots, at the time, in Winnipeg and in centers like Edmonton, and so on.
And there we just stayed there for a day and a night, and then they packed us in the car and drove us out to the place-- well, this is where you're going to live.
[LAUGHS]
Well, my-- my-- well, as I said, we had two breadwinners, my father and-- and-- and myself.
And-- and our first job, when we built up-- we, well, we put up with another family.
And we built a two-room house, half of-- of that family, half of the [INAUDIBLE].
But they-- they had four children, all under the age of six.
Well, our family, well I was obviously-- I was, uh, just past 16.
My-- my sister was old enough to-- to-- to take a job as a domestic on-- on-- the farm there.
And that was our first job, to work on the farm.
And then we waited-- the biggest job in the place like that then was the harvest time.
When came harvest time, then this-- uh, uh, Swartz, he was the-- the-- the settler there-- there, he-- he gave-- he gave us his address in Calgary-- he lived in Calgary.
When you come, come to harvest, you come to me.
Then-- then-- he was the-- he was the, uh, uh, Labor Office, who supplies the farmers with the harvest labors.
And in the prairie farms out-- out there.
You see there's this-- you know, prairies, it's like [INAUDIBLE] Alberta-- the smallest farm was a half section, which means it's 120 acres.
So he sent us to a-- to a farm.
And between the two of us, in the harvest and threshing time, we made a whole sum of $126.
[LAUGHS] That was a big [INAUDIBLE] in my day.
Well, that was the time.
And [INAUDIBLE] really deep, in [INAUDIBLE] of the Depression.

Well, in the-- well, we settled on that-- on that farm there, and built-- well, there-- there were people with-- in-- in Leduc-- it was 20 miles away-- away from the place where we-- and there were some Hungarians there.
There was a Hungarian-- a Hungarian blacksmith, and there was a Hungarian-- Hungarian shoemaker, in that region, in Leduc.
Well, you should remember, Leduc was the first place where the oil came out.
I used to work on a farm-- [LAUGHS] start the first-- the first oil.
And that blacksmith had a-- had a quarter section of land out there.
Well, he could afford it-- what would mind you-- afford a section of $4,600-- [INAUDIBLE] 160 acres land.
And through him, well, we, uh, got acquainted.
And then aligning in-- into Edmonton.
And so on in Edmonton, I was Hungarian community, already.
Oh, yeah, we worked in [INAUDIBLE].
So when we then-- when people found out in Edmonton-- mind you, leftist people-- [LAUGHS] found out, they came to visit us.
One was a shoemaker, the other-- other-- what-- what-- what was he?
He was-- he was-- he was a locksmith, I take, or something.
They came to visit on a bright June Sunday afternoon, came with this Leduc blacksmith-- he brought him out.
Well, these are the new arrivals in our Hungarian community.
Not-- not in Alberta.
And one of them, the shoemaker brought a copy of the Canadian Hungarian [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, so, in-- about three months in Canada, we already had-- I doubt they called it a communist paper, you see, but that's what it was.
See, it was narrated by communists in Toronto.
And it was speaking our language.
It was special-- my father, when he first read it, the first page, and he kissed it.
Well, well, I'm right at home.
[LAUGHS]
Uh, no he never belonged to any organization in-- in-- Hun-- well, he--
No, in Canada, I mean.
Oh-- oh, in Canada-- well, then, at that age-- at that age-- well, I was-- I was in the frontline, already, as far as the organization, and so on.
Because I-- I was the younger, and I-- I used to go [INAUDIBLE] on hiking, and so on, and so.
When that, we had an organization, a Hungarian organization, a leftist organization-- cultural, and [INAUDIBLE] organization, and so on.

And, uh, came a national organizer from-- from-- from Toronto-- he was on a-- on a tour in Western Canada-- and he heard about-- about this-- specially did this family, this harness-maker family, in Leduc, and he stopped.
And so in 19-- it was in 1933-- and then he just grabbed me and took me into Edmonton, right into the office of the communist boss.
[LAUGHS] I'm not hiding my-- my past, and that's how it-- that's how it happened.
Yeah.
And as young as that-- and then for the harvest time, Calgary was the [INAUDIBLE] of our-- next year, 1931, we went to-- farther into-- to-- for the harvest again.
And then we got acquainted with the-- with the Hungarian organization.
It was much, much bigger than-- than in Edmonton.
Because Calgary was a central-- there was a Drumheller-- [INAUDIBLE] the coal mines there.
There were 63 coal mines in Drumheller-- Drumheller [INAUDIBLE].
And so only work in the winter time, and in summer, all these miners came into Calgary.
The cowboy-- it was a cowboy town, and other agricultural workers.
And then--
Oh, yes, yes, yes-- from the paper, it was-- it was at that time, it was twice, twice a week, the paper.
We were very, very, well-- well-- well-informed, you see.
Well, the so-called-- the year, the international-- international Marxist movement, we all had a connection, and news-- news [INAUDIBLE] and so on, to the communist party here in Canada, in Toronto.
Well, there was no-- no problem of-- of-- of getting news first-hand.
And knew all about Hungary, and then Hitler-- definitely.
And-- and this-- this-- organization, the leftist organization was in it up to its-- its elbows, and up to its knees.
And-- and-- because like-- like in Edmonton-- since you asked me, you provoked me into it-- in that area, there was a lot of Germans-- German settlers, and so on.
And later, like in 1938, already, they organized a picnic that in all Saskatchewan, we were to flow into Edmonton-- there's a huge basin there.
And they organized a German picnic there-- 40,000 Germans came together, you see, and they were already, 'heil Hitler,' in that picnic, in 1938.
Oh, yes, yes, yes, yes-- they had-- they had big plans.
Hitler really had-- and all this-- all this gangsters around him.
What-- what-- what they preach, they really believed it.
They're going to conquer the world for the next 1,000 years.
And you could-- you could-- you could see it there.

And my father, well, he spoke German better than-- than Hungarian-- he spoke Slovak-- and-- he-- he-- his customer-- in, when he opened up a harness shop in Leduc, then the surrounding area was very good land-- cleared land from-- from forest.
And as thick as the forest was, so-- so was the wheat, grown as thick as that.
And most of them were-- they were all good Germans from Russia, and-- and from-- from-- from-- [INAUDIBLE] so was in Saskatchewan.

Not the--the funniest-- the funniest thing, even up to-- up to this day, the Germans are the [INAUDIBLE], and mind you there are well over 1 million of them here-- but we always had ethnic organizations, like-- like in the leftist-- 17, 18 such organizations.
Even-- even the Germans-- but the Germans always the smallest.
But hardly-- hardly any [NON-ENGLISH] Dutch.
Most of them were the [NON-ENGLISH], who came from Hungary, Austria, or Romania, and so on.
And some Austrians.
But no high-German.
Not the elite, you mean.
No, no, no-- they were kept to themselves, and they were so rich, and so strong.
But you could hardly see them, you could hardly see-- but, uh, you were there among them.
You would rub shoulders with them.
And so the customers in my father's shop-- in that little [INAUDIBLE].
There was vulgar Germans-- rich farmers and--

Uh, well, right-- right there in Leduc-- I'm not-- not really knowing exactly, because my-- my profession was that I was an organizer at that time-- a professional organizer-- for our-- our movement, our organization.
And I came down for a convention which was planned for October, to be held in Hamilton.
But they called me down early-- a month early-- to help to organize the convention.
I arrived here on a CPR train on-- on Saturday morning, that was-- it was 28th of August, 1939.
And the exhibition was still on the last day, there was no exhibition of Sundays.
And they took me out to see the exhibition.
And then came Sunday, the 31st of August-- and they did put up-- put me up with a Hungarian family there.
Monday morning, I was going down to the-- to the editorial office, and all-- all of a sudden, I had a call-- Andy!
Andy!
And there I see [NON-ENGLISH] a Hungarian guy, who was in our organization-- I already knew him-- he was delivering bread, you see.
He said, do know what-- what-- what the news is?
What?
He said, the war broke out-- at 4 o'clock, a German invaded Poland.
